insights:
Nutrition note: only 3 days of food data exist, so no reliable dietary patterns can be drawn. The findings below focus on Garmin variables where 27–30 days of signal are available.

1. Poor Sleep Score Predicts Elevated Next-Day RHR: On the four nights with sleep scores at or below 51 (May 2, 3, 6, 25), the following morning's RHR averaged 51 bpm. On nights scoring 82 or above, next-morning RHR averaged 47.5 bpm. That 3.5 bpm gap is consistent enough across the month to suggest sleep quality — not just duration — is the stronger driver of cardiac recovery.

2. Deep Sleep Collapse Accompanies the Worst Sleep Scores: The three lowest-scoring nights (May 20 at 46, May 23 at 44, May 25 at 45) all show deep sleep at 1.1 hours or below — May 11 hit 0.8 hours, the dataset minimum. Nights scoring above 82 average 1.75 hours of deep sleep. Duration alone doesn't explain this: May 20 reached 5.6 hours total but only 1.1 deep, while May 26 hit 12.6 hours with 1.8 deep. Something is disrupting sleep architecture specifically, not just cutting sleep short.

3. High Step Days Cluster With Elevated Stress and Worse Recovery: The five highest step days (May 8 at 16,055, May 10 at 14,575, May 28 at 12,854, May 2 at 13,370, May 19 at 11,573) show an average stress score of 43. The five lowest step days average a stress score of 29. Causality is ambiguous — stress may drive movement or movement may accumulate stress — but the two variables clearly travel together and both predict the next morning's RHR trend.

4. May 28 Run Shows a Concerning Effort Spike: The May 28 run logged an average HR of 179 bpm at a pace of 7:56 min/km — the slowest pace of any run yet the highest cardiac output. By contrast the May 19 run held 172 bpm at 5:49 pace, and the May 31 run held 168 bpm at 6:33 pace. High HR at slow pace suggests the body was working significantly harder for less output, worth tracking as a potential marker of accumulated fatigue or inadequate pre-run recovery.

5. Stress Spike on May 22 Is an Outlier Worth Flagging: May 22 recorded a stress score of 68 — the dataset peak, nearly 50% above the 30-day average of roughly 36 — with no sleep score recorded, suggesting either a measurement gap or severely disrupted night. The following morning's RHR was 54 bpm, the highest in the dataset. This date warrants recollection of any external event that could explain the combined anomaly.
